Hi, Jordan Belfort here, and now I want to welcome you to the script building technology section of the straight line system. I think of all the different sections of the straight line technology, this is perhaps in its own way the most important because what a script really does, it's the well thought out essence of the perfect sale. In other words, it allows you essentially to go to all the past experiences you have with selling a product and to give you all your best lines in exactly the right order and it basically allows you to keep complete control of the sale and to sound like you know what you're talking about it. In other words, it allows you to answer objections in a way that doesn't take you off the straight line and off into Pluto Uranus, right? It keeps you in that comfort zone where you're in control of the sale and from a company's perspective, meaning management, it allows them to systemize their sales force and manage it all in the same fashion. That's crucial to a company's uniformity and really allows them to grow and expand and duplicate things in a way that if they walk into one office and there's another office or another office, everyone's doing things the same precise way. That's important not just for sales for results, it's also important for ethical reasons, for regulatory reasons because in the absence of scripts what I've found very often is that sales will start going off the straight line and saying things sometimes that are really, really stupid and sometimes that are really, really not true. Some salesmen do that no doubt and they're bed intentioned but I would say more often than not, perhaps 90% or better of the time, it's not so much that the salesman wants to lie or be deceitful, they just run out of intelligent things to say. Once they run out of intelligent things to say, they start making it up. They think it sounds good as they're saying it but when you actually on the other end listen to it, it's hard on the ears. What it really does is it gives you the opportunity to really go through an entire sale from A to Z and script out all of the rebuttals. You really know all the objections beforehand because there's only, let's say, five or six different rebuttals that people ever come up with and this allows you to create an airtight, logical and an airtight emotional case to overcome people's objections, to also create those three 10s I spoke about on the product yourself and your company and allows you to make those airtight, logical and emotional cases without lying, exaggerating or wasting your prospects time. It also allows you to make effective use of tonality in body language. Remember only 9% of human communication is the actual words. A lot more of it is how you say your words, the tone of what you're saying, how you move your body when you're face to face. What it chunks up to at the higher levels is that you're a person worth listening to, that you're a person that can help them achieve their goals, help them get their own outcomes when you have certain tone eyes, let's say a tonality of absolute certainty. So let me give you a certainty for one second here. I say, let me tell you something, you do not understand how incredible this product is. Now those words themselves are stupid, but let's just say it this way. I can't tell you, you have no idea how incredible this product is. Now I say, I can't tell you how incredible this product is. Which one sounds more compelling? Now of course the words are kind of ridiculous, but you saw that when I applied a tonality of certainty to the same set of words, it took on a much greater meaning and it starts to move you emotionally. Another thing I want you to notice is that when I started talking about, you know, the script being, and I was very certain, I started sounding certain, notice how I started moving my hands. I started smacking the back of my right hand into my left palm. That's a gesture that I use to create certainty. Now it happens automatically, but for your purposes, as you're learning to muster that certain tone whenever you want it, you'll notice that every person, especially you, you know, for your own purposes, you have a gesture that you'll do automatically when you want to summon the tone of certainty. So once you identify that gesture, which mine is this, right, if you need to sound certain and you know there's a certain spot in your presentation you want to be absolutely certain about, you can create that tone and assist yourself in creating that tone by using the gesture in this case, it would be this. So I'd say, watch, you know, this is the most incredible product that we've ever released or say this is the most incredible product we've ever released. Again, you saw much more power with the latter versus the former. And you notice how I automatically bang my hand into the back of my hand. And for me, so you know, that's my gesture. You might have an entirely different gesture. So what I'm going to ask you to do, and this is also in other elements of the straight line system, you learn how to link certain gestures to your tonalities. But for the purposes of script building, remember, you're going to see as you, once you have a great script that has a, you know, a complete introduction, a body in a close, you're going to see that there are certain specific spots and you need to sound very certain other spots. We need to have a tone of sincerity, a tone of reasonableness, whatever those different tones will go through all those tones in a moment. But what you'll see is that once you know, when you have a script, you know where they are, you can use them and summon them at the precise moment. Okay. Now why, the question really is why my scripts and what are really special about the scripts I write and, you know, are they better than other scripts? Are they different? And the answer is, well, I think they're a hell of a lot better than other scripts, but most importantly, they're structured in a way that's formulaic. In other words, they can be duplicated for any industry, for any outcome that you want. And I want to give you some very basic structure here, some things that allow you really to adopt your industry to the straight line system. And one of the most important things, remember about any script is that, and this goes by the, let me just digress for a second, the Hollywood, because you know, there's a, you know, the Wolf Wolf Wall Street movie with the Alina Art of the Caprio, right? And the script for that movie, when you read the script, because it's a phenomenal script, it doesn't sound like the actors are reading from a script. There's the lines are written in such a way that it sounds natural and it's pleasing to the ear. An important quality to any script. See, you can never sound like you're reading from a script. So you have to learn to master the art. And again, it's a straight line technology to learn to master the art of reading a script without sounding like you're reading a script. And that could be in person because of course you don't have a script in front of you when you're selling face to face or influencing face to face. And when you're on the phone, yes, you can have one, but either way, the most important thing is that you sound natural. And the words that you say, the syllables, the number of beats and the rhythm is pleasing to the ear. OK, so that's one thing that's very, very important in my scripts. Another thing is the scripts that I write are not what's called front loaded. And what I mean by that is that you don't put every single benefit and every single feature right up front. And you're just like you try to blast the person and try to get them to, you know, these super high levels of certainty right away. What happens is typically the way we're built as human beings is that you can't get someone from zero to 100 like that. There has to be these little stopping off points. You know, you get someone who is maybe not that certain about your product or you. And then you run a language pattern, you go through part of a script and they become more certain. And then you back off and then you can again re approach them again, make them even more certain. You back off again a little bit later in the conversation, you make them even more certain notes. You can raise someone's level of certainty up bit by bit. You can't get it all at once. So what happens is very often the mistake I see in poorly written scripts with people that don't know the straight line technique is the front load. They put all the great stuff up front. Then when the person inevitably says, let me think about it, they have nothing else to say to convince that person to influence that person that now is the best time to make the decision. So it's very important not the front load. Okay. And also another thing about the scripts that I write as part of the straight line system is that each script is an integral part to an overall system. It's not that there's one script. There might be five or six different scripts that you go through from the entire process from A to Z, from open to close. You have your main, you have your qualifying script, you have your, your central body of your presentation, then after you have your rebuttal scripts. And the idea is you need to have four or five different very cohesive statements and what I want to call them wraps, so to speak, that you can make about your product, about yourself. And each statement that you make is designed to raise up someone's level of certainty about the three tens and at the same time to move them down the straight line from the open to the close. Let me just draw on the board here just so you can see the straight line, right? Like this is here. So what happens is, is while your main script might take you from here to here, let's say from here to here's your qualifying script, right? Here's your main script. And here's the script where you're dealing with rebuttals and objections here. Each of these scripts is integral to the straight line. If any point in time, you either number one, break rapport with it, with it, with the prospect, number two, say something that's just entirely ridiculous or it's a lie and they catch you in a lie, right? Or three, if you end up saying things that take the person out of the actual influence itself, you go off into Pluto, you start like, remember your boundaries here, you have above and below the line. If at any point you don't have your scripts and you start talking about the price of tea in China, things that aren't actually germane to the influencing situation, you lose, essentially you lose the ability to close the deal. So strips occur here, here and here. And every one of them is equally important. Okay. And when used together, you create that goal. You will say you can meet that goal, which I laid out early, which is to remember close anyone who's closeable, which doesn't mean everyone, but you close everyone who's closeable. Okay. Now let's talk about the different various elements of script writing. And number one, of course, is basic structure for a script. There are three main sections. Let me write them down here. You. And what you have, number one starts with your intro, your introduction. Okay. This is a short introduction. It's where you, if it's a cold call or, you know, someone that you're, you know, calling or you're in a store situation, someone walks in and see how you introduce yourself. Okay. Let's go out down here. Okay. That we're actually calling someone or, you know, walking in someone's door and trying to influence in them. Right. So you have your introduction first, then that's where you essentially transition from the, you know, the, you know, you introduce yourself and then you tell them the reason why you're there today. Okay. And then you ask them for permission to qualify them. That's how an introduction, in the short order, that's really how it's broken down to three sections. Let's go through it again. Section A is where you introduce yourself with a greeting. Section B is where you actually tell them the reason why you're there today, the reason why you're there. And C is you ask for permission to qualify. And that's your basic script for any sort of cold call, whether it's in person or over the telephone. Okay. From there, you transition into the main body of your script. This is number two is the body. And typically your body, okay, the transition from this to this from your intro to your body is that after you qualify someone and you uncover what their needs are, what their desires are, what their pain is, right. And you have a pretty good understanding of that A, the number one that this, that your product does fill their need. If it doesn't, you should end the call and you're not trying to close someone who shouldn't be closed. And you also understand that they're financially qualified. I mean that your product could meet all their needs, but they don't have the money. And you know, you don't want to really, it's not fair to really, you know, bat someone over the head that doesn't have money because you just make them feel like crap because they know that you can benefit them and help them yet. They're, they're, they're stuck in their box because of money. So you don't want to sit there, you know, banging your head against the wall, trying to close people who don't have money for your product. So the idea is part of your, in your qualifying section, your S permission to qualify here. And this is in a separate section of the straight line, you know, in your actual course where you learn what questions you should be asking the big picture questions and well down to the smaller picture questions and how you use that system from going from big to small to really identify all of somebody's needs, all their wants. And of course what their pain is and what really is missing from their life right now. And of course next step would be for your product to be or your service or your coaching program, whatever it might be that you're offering would fill those needs and help them get out of pain and into pleasure. Okay. Your transition from here to here is a very simple line, which is based on everything you just said to me, this program is a perfect fit for you. Follow me. So let me say that again. So based your transition would be, and you'll see this is written down in your manual, but just to understand verbally, you'll say, well, based on everything you just said to me, this is definitely a perfect fit for you. Now notice the difference there when I use tonality. When I just said it to you casually, watch his casual, well, based on everything you just said to me, this is definitely a perfect fit for you. That's way number one way. Number two, well, let me say this based on everything you just said to me, this is definitely a perfect fit for you. Which one has more power? The second operative word that was definitely, and I said it with a hard D. I was like, definitely a perfect fit for you. That again is the tone of certainty and also a tone of enthusiasm, a tone of confidence. Right. And you sound like what an expert.
